candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Northern Agenda The two huge billion-pound Greater Manchester projects at the heart of a new masterplan Atom Valley and MIX Manchester are set to be showcased to investors at major summit in Leeds - but all eyes will be on Andy Burnham Comments News R Two huge billion-pound innovation zones in Greater Manchester are at the heart of a new blueprint by the North of England's political leaders to unlock a "new era of prosperity" through investment and jobs At the Great North Investment Summit in Leeds today, Northern mayors will unveil a major pipeline of opportunities from across the region - all with the backing of Chancellor Rachel Reeves - in the hope of attracting investment from major businesses The prospectus going before business leaders features nine major projects, including the Atom Valley advanced manufacturing 'mega-cluster' north of Manchester and the MIX Manchester science and innovation campus next to Manchester Airport The two huge schemes have a combined potential value in excess of £2 billion and could create thousands of new jobs, local officials say, but more investment is needed for them to deliver on their promise Ahead of the summit, Ms Reeves said the pipeline of projects "shows the huge opportunity across the North – from thriving cities to world-leading industries that can bring in investment and support the jobs of the future"

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Real Life 'It's killed me to ask him if he wants to die, but he doesn't - he wants to fight' Sharon has not heard her husband Steven's voice since November 2024. He is bedbound and unable to move or speak after a diagnosis of locked-in syndrome. James News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 05:56, 18 May 2026 Steven Melia might not be able to move or speak, but he knows exactly what's going on around him. For more than a year he has been awake but bedbound, surrounded by the same four walls with wife Sharon firm It's been a gruelling battle since the 48-year-old data inputter was diagnosed with locked-in syndrome. The rare and serious neurological disorder causes paralysis of muscles. People with the condition are conscious, alert and have their usual cognitive abilities, but become unab Sharon remembers clearly the day she last heard his voice. Friday, November 1, 2024. But, the pair still communicate with each other every day He can either blink, or look at 'yes' or 'no' cards to indicate what he wants. Sharon frequently quizzes him and does brain exercises with him But after 18 months of treatment, she claims doctors are now discussing palliative care for Steven. Although there is no known cure or treatment to reverse the syndrome, intense rehabilitation and specialist care has been known to largely increase movement
candidates.draft_line: • Greater Manchester: 48-летний Стивен Мелия, страдающий синдромом запертого человека (locked-in syndrome), не слышал голоса жены Шэрон с ноября 2024 года. Он прикован к постели, не может двигаться и говорить, но общается с помощью моргания. Шэрон заявила, что врачи обсуждают паллиативную помощь, хотя Стивен хочет бороться.

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Politics The uncomfortable truth behind Greater Manchester’s local election results Women made up less than a third of councillors elected across Greater Manchester this year, with some warning abuse and intimidation are driving women away from politi Analysis by the Manchester Evening News revealed that men made up around 71 percent (152) of the 214 councillors, while women accounted for just 29 percent (62) This marks a sharp drop from just two years ago, when there was a 60-40 split between male and female councillors elected. In 2021, 44 percent of councillors elected were female These findings have led some to raise “alarm bells”, and call for more to be done to encourage women in politics, while tackling the abuse some of them face Oldham saw the lowest proportion of women elected this year, at just five percent – down from 40 percent in 2024. This was followed by Salford and Rochdale , where 20 percent of elected candidates were women – down from 50 percent and 25 percent respectively in 2024 Only Stockport and Trafford had better female representation, at 57 percent and 43 percent respectively. The imbalance was particularly stark among Reform UK councillors following the party’s breakthrough in Greater Manchester. Of the 106 councillors elected in the region last we

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Courts They cradled his head as he lay bleeding to death, knowing nothing of a brutal attack minutes earlier Vernard Murray lost his life following a savage attack in Cheetham Hill View 6 Images A single bunch of flowers at the scene where Vernard Mur News Amy Walker Court reporter 17:48, 17 May 2026 Updated 21:07, 17 May 2026 Cradling his head as he lay bleeding to death, members of a horrified family told Vernard Murray everything was going to be okay They comforted him, scrambling for towels at the direction of a 999 operator. "He has been stabbed," one of them told the call handler. "He's losing a lot of blood." Those around him were heard screaming Seconds earlier, the 26-year-old had clambered over their garden fence before dragging himself to their patio doors. He banged on the glass, desperate for help "The man was standing there," Tayyab Ali, who was visiting his grandmother, said. "He was mumbling something, but I couldn't understand what he was saying. He was trying to get in. He just looked like he was out of it." Growing weaker, Vernard pleaded: "Help me... help me." Lying prostrate, he rolled over. The reality of what Mr Ali was seeing became shockingly clear

As the cost of living crisis bites, students from all backgrounds are turning to a campus pantry handing out staples. Who can actually afford to be a student in Manchester these days? “I saved so much money in the first year.” The recent demolition of Ayad’s first year residence - a notoriously shoddy and famously lively setup - marked the end of an era Despite myriad flaws it was one of the most affordable student halls in Manchester, offering an option for those on the lowest incomes. The university’s Fallowfield campus is currently undergoing a major redevelopment that bosses say will deliver “seismic benefits” But with swanky new university accommodation having cropped up across the city in recent years, some question if it’s really what students need - or can afford A bed at Oak House started at £113 a week in 2024, according to The Mancunion . candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Strangeways 'Promising conversations' taking place over moving Strangeways prison outside of Greater Manchester There have long been calls for the infamous, crumbling Victoria jail to be closed View 3 Images The jail dates back to the Victorian era (I News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 16:18, 17 May 2026 Discussions have taken place over finally moving Strangeways the Manchester Evening News understands The high-security category A and B men's prison first opened its doors in 1868. It was renamed HMP Manchester after it was the scene of a now infamous 25-day riot and rooftop protest in 1990, sparked by its squalid conditions There have been calls spanning several decades for the jail housed in a crumbling Victorian site, which occupies almost 20 acres off Bury New Road at the northern tip of Manchester city centre , to be moved elsewhere In 2022, Manchester City Council wrote to the Government , arguing HMP Manchester was 'coming to the end of its natural lifespan' and was not suitable for the 'significant remodelling and expansion' needed to bring it up to modern-day standards The council leader, Coun Bev Craig, said its current location was acting as a ‘barrier to growth and development which could bring new jobs, much-needed houses and green space.’

After graduating in graphic design, he became a fashion buyer, dealing with suppliers from across the world for major brands He worked in Selfridges before embarking on a fashion buyer course, later moving to Manchester from London due to a promotion. His job was to communicate with suppliers and designers on a global scale But in 2024, he lost his job. candidates.evidence_text: News Hopwood Hall The fight over Hopwood Hall The long-running saga turned sour in 2024, when the council cut ties with the Hollywood filmaker View 8 Images Hopwood DePree News George Lythgoe Local Democracy Reporter 19:37, 17 May 2026 A spat between a Hollywood actor and Rochdale council over control of historic Hopwood Hall has raged for more than a year. Now they will battle it out in the high court Hopwood DePree was leading the redevelopment of the Grade II*-listed site in Middleton until the town hall cut ties with him in 2024. This long-running saga began over a decade ago when the American crossed the pond in a bid to restore what he believes to be his ancestral home In 2017 there was excitement that the site, which dates back to the 1400s, could finally be restored as the council entered into an ‘exclusivity agreement’ with Mr DePree. That exclusivity agreement came to an abrupt end on October 31, 2024 Mr DePree’s dream was shattered as the council cut ties with him so they could speak to other investors, which the exclusivity agreement prevented The local authority felt the US-born Middletonian had not made enough progress and ended the agreement. The 56-year-old American claims to have spent more than £750,000 on restoring the property, as well as putting in numerous hours alongside members of the local community

Совет расторг эксклюзивное соглашение с ДеПри в октябре 2024 года, посчитав прогресс недостаточным. ДеПри утверждает, что потратил более £750 000 на реставрацию.

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Strangeways How do you solve a problem like Strangeways? SPECIAL REPORT: It’s the Victorian prison that some say should have been shut 50 years ago. But is rebuilding it elsewhere now the only answer? Chris Slater spoke to experts to find out. View 10 Comments News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 07:00, 17 May 2026 Strangeways has long been shorthand for chaos, disorder and institutional decay, a place whose very name seems to carry the echo of its own troubled history Designed by Alfred Waterhouse, the architect behind Manchester Town Hall, the imposing Grade II-listed prison on Southall Street first opened its gates in June 1868. More than 150 years later, it still looms over the city, its Victorian architecture a reminder of a very different The prison’s reputation hardened into national notoriety in 1990 when it became the scene of what was then the longest prison riot in British penal history In the years that followed, around £90 million was poured into repairs and refurbishment, and in 1994 it was officially renamed HMP Manchester in an effort to draw a line under the past. Yet despite the investment and the rebranding, the underlying problems have proved stubbornly After an inspection in September and October 2024, the prison was effectively placed in special measures with inspectors issuing an 'urgent notification' which required the Government to develop a response and action plan within 28 days

candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Bury How Labour held off Reform in Bury Bury bucked the trend for Labour as the party faced a dismal local elections elsewhere View 8 Images Bury backed Labour in the local elections - but there was a sense of apathy among voters (Image: Manchester Ev News Stephen Topping Senior reporter and Hannah Richardson 16:18, 17 May 2026 It was the 'kicking' from voters that signalled a week of turbulence in Westminster. Across Greater Manchester and beyond, voters shunned Labour in their droves The ordeal for Labour has left huge question marks over the future direction of the country. Dozens of MPs have called on Sir Keir Starmer to go, while the Westminster rumour mill has been in overdrive regarding who might replace him, including Greater Manchester mayor Andy Burnh Yet in one corner of Greater Manchester, Labour truly bucked the trend. Bury saw some of the party's best results anywhere in the country in last week's local elections For council leader Eamonn O'Brien, who comfortably defended his seat of St Mary's in Prestwich , the election results were the fruits of several months' hard work that followed a wake-up call given a few miles up the A56. When Lancashire county council flipped to Reform in 2025, "We knew going into the campaign that it was going to be tough," Coun O'Brien told the Manchester Evening News . "We looked at the results in the county council elections last year - Lancashire's just up the road from Bury - and the results there were bad for Labour
candidates.draft_line: • Bury: лейбористы в Бери показали лучшие результаты на местных выборах, удержав позиции, несмотря на общенациональный спад. Лидер совета Имон О'Брайен, защитивший своё место в St Mary's, Prestwich, объяснил успех работой после поражения в Ланкашире в 2025 году. Явка была низкой, но партия смогла удержать Reform UK.

candidates.lead: News Greater Manchester News Courts 28 shipments came in from the US - there wasn't just vintage clothing inside Fake companies, massive deliveries and millions of pounds in profit View 8 Images Dean Hussain, Prince Genesis Oyewo and Elliot McBurnie (Image: Greater Manchester Pol
candidates.evidence_text: News Greater Manchester News Courts 28 shipments came in from the US - there wasn't just vintage clothing inside Fake companies, massive deliveries and millions of pounds in profit View 8 Images Dean Hussain, Prince Genesis Oyewo and Elliot McBurnie (Image: Greater Manchester Pol News Amy Walker Court reporter 17:35, 13 May 2026 On the face of it, Dean Hussain was running a legitimate, growing business. The owner of multiple companies involved in the importation of vintage clothing from the United States, the 26-year-old appeared to have a bright future In reality, Hussain was the 'controlling mind' behind a massive smuggling gang that imported at least 675kg of cannabis into the UK. Numerous fake companies - set up using stolen or fake identities - placed orders with outfits in California Concealed within huge shipments of clothing was masses of Cali weed, a powerful strain of the drug. Minshull Street Crown Court heard that between December 2022 and January 2024, 28 shipments came into the country Five were intercepted by Border Force officials, who found nearly 130kg of cannabis said to have been worth £902,210 at wholesale price and £3.7m at street prices "On the basis that the remaining 23 shipments contained a similar amount of cannabis, the total value of the cannabis imported in the 28 shipments was up to £5.3m if sold in wholesale bulk quantities, and up to £19.3m if sold at street level," prosecutor Adam Birkby said

candidates.evidence_text: What's On What's On News Royal Exchange Theatre 'Manchester changed forever after what happened in 1996 - adversity brought people together' The impact of the Manchester IRA bomb forms part of a unique new production View 6 Images The aftermath of the IRA bomb in Manchester in 19 What's On Adam Maidment What's On and LGBTQ+ Writer 07:26, 17 May 2026 At 11.17am on June 15, 1996, a powerful 1,500 kg bomb detonated on Manchester’s Corporation Street. Whilst there were no fatalities, the explosion - executed by the IRA - injured more than 212 people and cause Harry Spooner, 75, was in town with his nephew and sister-in-law that day attending a Star Trek exhibition at the Science and Industry museum. Whilst exploring a faithful recreation of the bridge of the Starship Enterprise, the sound of the explosion rippled through the venue - n “There were various expletives from people at the time, but we all just thought the special effects were really amazing,” Harry, from Rusholme, recalls to the Manchester Evening News. “The building just shook - and we all thought it was so realistic. We thought we were being atta Harry, a former secretary for The Teachers' Union, and his family then made their way down to Liverpool Street, where he recalls they were handed drinks by staff at the nearby restaurants as people lined up by the telephone boxes to ring loved ones back at home to find out what h “I do recall feeling like it was all quite strange,” he explained. “This was before the days of mobile phones and there was a lot of confusion. You could see this big plume of smoke up in the air, it was quite frightening. We were told we had to get out of the city centre.”

Years of speculation about Andy Burnham finding a seat to make his way back to Westminster, and he now has one: Makerfield, where the sitting Labour MP Josh Simons announced he would step down just two years after being elected, to trigger a by-ele It’s a massive gamble for Burnham, but one commensurate with the prize. If he wins, he will likely be the favourite to win a Labour leadership race and become prime minister. But to get there, several hurdles stand in his way, including being approved to stand by Labour’s Nationa Will Burnham win in Makerfield? He should do... Polls show he is much more popular than Starmer or the Labour Party brand, and he’s lived in that neck of the woods for years so local people know him well. He must have calculated that he’s more likely to win in a suburban seat whe Ophira is wandering around Makerfield (which takes in Ashton-in-Makerfield, Platt Bridge and Hindley plus lots of Wigan suburbs like Winstanley and Hawkley) today to chat to voters. It will be interesting to see how Burnham answers questions about the EU in his campaign (Makerfie We will be covering the by-election with gusto until polling day, but for today’s edition, we’re looking forward to a Burnham-less future in Greater Manchester. candidates.evidence_text: The Mill investigates a series of mudslinging anonymous email accounts ahead of next week's local elections Hey! Jack Dulhanty here , staff writer at The Mill. Our local election coverage aims to take readers deeper into the contests for council seats across Greater Manchester, focussing on key characters who are having big impacts on the city region’s politics. Today’s piece is on a f In late February, not long after the death of England’s longest serving councillor, David Lancaster, Salford’s Labour councillors received an email. It was to confirm that a by-election would be held for Lancaster’s seat — Barton and Winton — barely two weeks before the local ele This was unusual. When a seat is vacated so close to a local election, normal practice is to put it to the polls alongside the other seats set to be contested. But, if at least two members of an opposition party call for it, the council is compelled to put on a by-election. This Sign up for The Mill Manchester's quality newspaper, delivered via email “In all my years it has never happened,” says Barbara Bentham, a councillor in Salford who worked closely with Lancaster for years. “We usually wait until after the funeral.” Indeed, Lancaster’s family had hardly begun arranging his funeral when the by-election was called. In fact, it was a lot cheaper Edwards and her partner, who live in Portugal, had bought their property — in the Northern Quarter’s Market Buildings — just six months before for around £170,000. It was intended as a place for them to stay while visiting their elderly parents in the UK. But the flat Edwards had Her eyes flitted around the listing trying to figure out what was going on. “It was frighteningly cheap,” she told me over the phone recently. After a while, unable to fathom how a flat that was essentially the same as hers was suddenly selling for so much less, she decided to co Edwards spoke to a man named Adrian who told her the flat was so cheap because the building had cladding issues. Edwards told him she had just bought a place in the same development, and wasn’t aware of cladding issues. Flustered, Adrian told her he’d get some more information an But when she checked, the listing was still there. candidates.evidence_text: A number of former pupils have gone on to become published authors Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Carolyn O'Brien with four books published by former Loreto pupils, including her own new novel Rose & Renzo Walk into Loreto Grammar School's newly refurbished library at lunchtime and you'll usually find it to be, in headteacher Mrs Beever's wor It's the kind of detail that goes some way to explaining why so many of its former pupils have gone on to become published authors Take Carolyn O'Brien, an author and current Loreto parent, whose debut novel The Song of Peterloo already won admirers for its vivid historical storytelling Her follow-up, Rose & Renzo , is due for publication by Northodox Press in May. Set in Manchester in 1936, it weaves together personal drama and the looming shadow of fascism, as aspiring artist Rose is swept up in the political storms of the era Then there is Louisa Reid, a current teacher at the school, whose novels for young adults have garnered national acclaim. Nominated four times for the prestigious Carnegie Medal, she has also been shortlisted for the Branford Boase Award and several regional prizes

candidates.evidence_text: The former independent school closed in July 2023 Share on WhatsApp Share via Email Trafford Council has confirmed it is progressing the purchase of the former Loreto Prep site on Dunham Road - a move that would allow North Cestrian School to expand by up to 300 places The acquisition is going ahead subject to due diligence, the council has confirmed, with the deal set to be funded through a capital grant from the Department for Education It marks the latest step in a long-running effort to secure extra secondary school places in Altrincham, where demand has far outstripped supply for several years. North Cestrian received over 1,500 applications for just 165 places in a recent admissions cycle, with around 60 fam The three-acre Dunham Road site became available after Loreto Prep - an independent Catholic primary school established in 1909 - closed in July 2023 Its trustees, the Loreto Sisters, cited declining pupil numbers and what they described as "unprecedented financial challenges" stemming partly from the pandemic and the wider economic climate. It is a region defined by a strong sense of place, deep and mature partnership working, and a shared commitment t “It has been a real honour to lead mental health, learning disability, autism and community services across Lancashire and South Cumbria over the past six years. candidates.lead: A pioneering Greater Manchester mental health programme has been named a regional champion at the first ever NHS Excellence Awards, recognising outstanding work to reduce out of area mental health placements and keep people closer to home The ‘Care Closer, Care Smarter: Transform
candidates.evidence_text: A pioneering Greater Manchester mental health programme has been named a regional champion at the first ever NHS Excellence Awards, recognising outstanding work to reduce out of area mental health placements and keep people closer to home The ‘Care Closer, Care Smarter: Transforming Mental Health Flow in Greater Manchester’ programme - delivered in partnership by Greater Manchester Mental Health NHS Foundation Trust (GMMH), NHS Greater Manchester , and Pennine Care NHS Foundation Trust - was named regional winner The programme takes a system wide approach to tackling out of area placements by using a shared fund, enabling partners to take collective responsibility for acute mental health inpatient care, reinvest savings into local services, and support people in their own communities wher Since the programme began, the impact has been significant Keeping people closer to home has improved continuity of care, supported recovery, and reduced inequalities across the system The collaborative approach has also delivered substantial financial benefits

candidates.evidence_text: Working with our service users, people and partners we have made significant progress on our improvement journey and we remain committed to continuously improving our services to ensure we provide safe, high-quality patient care which contributes to improving lives in Greater Man In January 2024 NHS England commissioned an Independent Review into the failings within the Trust’s services, reported at the Edenfield Centre, and the failure within the organisation to appropriately manage concerns and mitigate against patient harm. This followed concerns raise The Assurance Review published today by NHS England follows the publication of the initial Independent Review and focusses on the actions progressed and implemented in response to the recommendations made in that review. It provides an important moment for us to reflect on how fa The review team has recognised positive progress across every area they examined; this has been made possible by the hard work and commitment shown every day by colleagues across the Trust The Trust was given a progress rating of “Action significantly progressed” in nine out of the eleven areas which is exactly where an organisation at our stage of recovery should be. This rating means that the foundations are in place and actions are well under way. It also means The Assurance Review focusses on the actions that have been progressed and implemented in response to the recommendations made in the Independent Review. The report highlights areas where progress is already visible

candidates.evidence_text: This week (11 – 17 May) is Mental Health Awareness Week 2026. This year’s theme, ‘action’, focuses on taking action to support good mental health – for yourself, for someone else, for all of us To mark the week, we are sharing activities, advice, support and resources, alongside real-life examples and inspiration of how to take action for your mental health. Even small actions can make a big difference Our Talking Therapies services offer evidenced-based psychological interventions to support people experiencing common mental health problems including anxiety, depression and stress. They are available to anyone over the age of 16 who lives in Bolton, Salford, Manchester, Traffo Visit our Talking Therapies page to find out more, including how you can refer yourself Find your local Talking Therapies service on the NHS website if you live outside of the boroughs we cover Since childhood, Tom struggled with a phobia that had an impact on his life. Now 37, Tom shares how he is managing this, with some (rather unconventional) support from Trafford Talking Therapies

candidates.evidence_text: The British Medical Association (BMA) has announced that resident doctors (previously known as junior doctors) across England will take part in strike action from 7am on Tuesday 07 April until 7am on Monday 13 April Any GMMH patient or service user whose appointment is affected by the strike action will be contacted directly. If you are not contacted, we look forward to welcoming you for your appointment as planned During these strikes, other doctors (including consultants and other specialist doctors) will still be working. Staff will continue to provide patients with the best possible care under the circumstances, however, there is likely to be disruption to local services As with previous periods of strike action, we have tried and tested plans in place to mitigate risks to patient safety and manage any disruption We will continue to do everything we can to go ahead with planned appointments – especially for patients in greatest clinical need. Patients should attend their appointment as planned if they have not been contacted Regardless of any strike action taking place, it is really important that patients who need urgent medical care continue to come forward as normal, especially in emergency and serious life-threatening cases - when someone is seriously ill or injured, or their life is at risk
candidates.previous_title: Strike action taking place in April 2026 | News and Events

candidates.evidence_text: Manchester City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city Almost 1,000 of the 4,766 new homes completed across the city in 2025/26 were affordable, with 439 of these 901 affordable homes available for social rent This represents the most successful year since the mid-1990s for social and affordable home building in the city but there is much more to come as momentum accelerates There are around 1,450 further council, social and genuinely affordable homes already on site now with around 2,000 more such homes ready to start, 2026/27 looks set to be another bumper year The Council set out its 10-year housing strategy in 2022 with a bold commitment to help deliver 36,000 homes across across all tenures and areas of the city with at least 10,000 of these social, council or genuinely affordable homes, and at least 3,000 of these in the city centre With 2,430 such affordable homes already completed and a strong pipeline of future projects already either under construction, with planning permission and being readied for site, or on the horizon, there is every confidence that these targets will be met or exceeded

candidates.evidence_text: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear and simple information about what these changes mean in Stockport, and where you can get advice and support. Some local details are still being finalised, and we’ll add updates as soon as they are confirmed. For more information, you can also We understand how hard it can be waiting for an assessment, and how difficult it can be to know where to turn for help in the meantime. That’s why we’ve introduced a new way of getting early support from the moment you first notice signs that your child or young person may be aut We know this is a lot to take in. If you’d like to find out more, ask questions, or talk things through, you’re very welcome to come along to one of our information drop-in sessions The Strategic Lead for Neurodivergence in Stockport will be attending the following drop-in sessions supported by the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS), Parents and Carers Together Stockport (PACTStockport), and Stockport Parents of ADHD Children in Education (SP In-person session with CAMHS and PACTStockport on Monday 11th May 10.30am-12pm, at Alma Lodge, 149 Buxton Road, Stockport, SK2 6EL. Refreshments will be provided. Please reserve a spot for this session

He was inspired by Liverpool’s success as the 2008 European Capital of Culture This year is Oldham’s turn, and its successful bid “Rising Up” celebrates the borough’s rich heritage, diverse communities and growing creative sector, with activity taking place across new and existing cultural venues and public spaces The town receives a £50,000 grant from the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) Culture Fund to develop a programme of cultural events and activities celebrating its unique heritage and bringing new opportunities for residents and visitors The programme will be built around three key themes

candidates.lead: More memorial plaques were stolen from the memorial gardens at Overdale Crematorium over the weekend, bringing the total to over 900
candidates.evidence_text: More memorial plaques were stolen from the memorial gardens at Overdale Crematorium over the weekend, bringing the total to over 900 Staff from Bolton Council, which cares for the memorial gardens, discovered the theft early on Monday morning and have determined that 114 metal plaques were stolen sometime between 6pm on Friday and 7am on Monday The matter has been reported to the police, who are investigating The theft follows a similar incident in July 2024 when over 800 memorial plaques were stolen overnight from the gardens Council officers believe that even though the plaques are made of gunmetal, which is very low value compared with brass and other metals, the thieves may try to sell them to scrap metal merchants However, tighter rules governing scrap metal dealers make selling the plaques much more difficult, as anyone supplying scrap to a merchant must provide photo ID and a bank account, as payment in cash is illegal
candidates.previous_title: More memorial plaques stolen from Overdale Gardens - Bolton Council

candidates.evidence_text: Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence at the gallery where her practice has been influenced through the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programme, and visitors Venture Arts artist Sally Hirst is currently an Artist in Residence in our Everyday Art School gallery where her practice is influenced by the Whitworth’s exhibitions, collections, programmes, and visitors Sally is a socially engaged illustrator, textile artist and ceramicist with much of her work being inspired by the people around her Sally is particularly interested in social history and retelling the stories of ordinary people from the past. These have included Holocaust survivors, ‘witches’ and Victorian factory workers. She likes to link these stories to her own experiences, such as fostering refugees and Accessibility is at the heart of everything Sally creates. Such as writing audio descriptions in the form of poetry, writing easy reads, and delivering neurodivergent friendly tours You can find Sally most Thursdays in our Everyday Art School space on our Mezzanine

candidates.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

My show is about hope. It's also about mental illness, good liars, bad ideas, Lorraine Kelly and a gang lord forcing me to hold a raw chicken. But it's mostly about hope. I live with a complex mental illness called borderline personality disorder (BPD) and I s Joe Tracini is an actor, writer and TV presenter. He’s starred in TV shows including Hollyoaks, Coming of Age and The Great Outdoors, and was the host of the BAFTA winning The Dengineers on CBBC Since March 2020 his social media videos have been viewed over 90 million times. The BAFTA-nominated Channel 4 documentary Me and the Voice in My Head explored the frequently debilitating effects that Joe’s Borderline Personality Disorder has on every aspect of his life. His book “This isn’t just stand-up - it’s an emotional ride, wrapped up in wit, charm and the occasional 7 foot man with a raw chicken!” - British Theatre Guide “A tender mix of comedy and storytelling where the humour is soft, unhurried and all the more powerful for it. candidates.evidence_text: For International Mother Language Day 2026 People’s History Museum launched Banner Bingo in an additional seven languages Alongside English, visitors can take their pick from Arabic, Chinese, French, Persian, Polish, Spanish, and Urdu as they take part in the free Family Friendly activity at the museum Banner Bingo is a quest to find a series of images featured within the colourful banners that are on display throughout the museum’s galleries. You can pick up a Banner Bingo card from the welcome desk, and after completing your search there is a small prize for taking part. Two Part of a programme of activities organised by Manchester City of Literature to celebrate International Mother Language Day The translations of Banner Bingo were provided by Peak Translations Ltd, thebigword, The Translation People and the Association of Translation Companies Are you more independent or community focused? candidates.evidence_text: PHM’s headline exhibition for 2026, On The Line: 100 years of strikes & solidarity (on show until 2 November 2026), is accompanied by an immersive programme of events exploring the exhibition’s themes and the stories represented in PHM’s nationally recognised collection Find out more about the exhibition and the full programme of events below Step into our new Family Friendly Strike Den where you can dress up, speak up, read stories, and create protest art, inspired by strikes and solidarity explored throughout the museum Explore strikes and solidarity and create textiles inspired by your discussions in this artist led workshop You are invited to power up your imagination and build your own mini robot from a simple sheet of paper! Inspired by the Deceptioncon robot costume currently on display in On The Line , which once stood on the picket line in support of workers’ rights Explore a century of struggles and stories of strength drawing on the museum’s nationally significant collections. This free exhibition is part of the national commemorations, marking 100 years since the 1926 General Strike

candidates.evidence_text: Dough Religion x Two Cents Pizza House of Social Manchester, 10 Sir Howard Bernstein Approach, Manchester M15 4ND Thu 21st May, 2026 From 6pm Dough Religion and Two Cents Pizza are coming together for one night only, serving up unlimited slices inspired by three of the East Coast’s greatest pizza styles at House of Social Nic from Two Cents Pizza, one of the UK’s most respected pizza chefs and a multi-award-winning name in the scene, will be joining the Dough Religion team to co-host the evening, guiding guests through New Haven, New York and Detroit-style pizza in one very carb-heavy sitting Expect OG Tomato Pie, New York vodka slices with sausage or without, and Detroit-style squares topped with pepperoni, mascarpone and hot honey, or wild mushroom, mascarpone and truffle honey. There’ll also be New York cheesecake to finish, because subtlety is not the point here Each slice has been built around premium ingredients and collaborators including Petra Flour, Properoni and Casa Marrazzo, with live slicing, chef interaction and introductions to each style throughout the night They’re also launching their own East Coast Pale with Weekend Project for the occasion. Every ticket includes unlimited slices, a free can on arrival and access to a pop-up bar pouring a mini East Coast-style beer takeover

candidates.evidence_text: After captivating the nation’s heart as the winner of Britain’s Got Talent 2017, and announcing the imminent release of his debut album ‘Our Generation’ on Syco Music November 17th, multi-instrumentalist, composer and producer, Tokio Myers recently announced a show at The Albert Tokio Myers is totally unique. He’s created a sound all his own, fusing an array of musical influences that have shaped him as an artist, ranging from classical and cinematic to hip-hop, dance and 90s’ soul Tokio grew up in North London before being awarded a coveted piano scholarship at the Royal College of Music. A stint as a studio musician led to recording and touring with some of the biggest names in music including Amy Winehouse, The Police, Kanye West and Mr Hudson. Now those Tokio has recently released the beautiful single ‘Angel’ from his hotly anticipated album ‘Our Generation’ and it is absolutely gorgeous!

candidates.evidence_text: Nathaniel Rateliff is a striking, emotive tenor vocalist and songwriter whose work runs the gamut from simple folk and Americana to roots rock & roll to vintage rhythm & blues. He and his band the Night Sweats are based in Denver, Colorado. After fronting two earlier bands, Ratel Rateliff had begun writing songs that required a band, so he formed the Night Sweats. Their rowdy retro sound was deeply influenced by vintage soul, R&B and gospel traditions, Van Morrison and the Band. A formidable live unit with a reputation for stage-quaking shows, they signed Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats’ self-titled debut album was issued in August 2015, preceded by appearances at the Newport Folk Festival and The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon. They toured globally in support of the album. While still on the road, they issued A Little S Be sure to catch Nathaniel Ratecliff and The Night Sweats live at Albert Hall on the 14th April 2018!

Starters will include hogget mince on dripping toast, dressed Devon crab salad, roast lamb with gentleman’s relish, and English asparagus with a soft-boiled egg For mains, there’s ox cheek pie with Ratte potato mash, salmon with trout roe beurre blanc and mussels, a beef burger in a milk bun, and pork collar with cabbage and bacon Puddings keep things traditional too, with sticky toffee pudding and a pistachio and cherry Bakewell tart, a nod to co-owner Jamie Pickles’ Peak District roots. Sundays, as you’d hope, will revolve around a solid roast. Expect rump of beef, porchetta, roast chicken and Knutsford

candidates.evidence_text: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership At Manchester Digital, some of the most useful conversations start very simply - with a member raising a challenge they are facing in their business and asking, “Are others thinking about this too?” That was exactly the case with our recent roundtable on value-based pricing in digital agencies The session came about after a conversation with one of our members, who was exploring how agencies are approaching value-based pricing models and whether others in the community were asking similar questions. It quickly became clear that this wasn’t an isolated issue. As more co So, as part of our commitment to creating meaningful opportunities for members to connect around shared challenges, we brought together a small group of agency leaders for an honest, practical discussion focused on pricing, commercial models and the wider pressures shaping agency We were delighted to be joined by Richard Gregory (Agency NXD), who set the scene with a practical and thought-provoking overview of value-based pricing, drawing on his experience working with agencies and tech businesses

candidates.evidence_text: By Kate Wilson, Head of Community & Membership, Manchester Digital The question of how to approach AI in coding interviews is one many employers are now grappling with, but very few feel they have fully answered This topic first came up in a conversation I had with Geraint North at Arm , who highlighted a tension that will feel familiar to many. Candidates are increasingly using AI tools in their day to day work and education, yet interview processes often still expect them to operate wi It felt like the kind of question that didn’t have a simple answer, but would really benefit from bringing people together. So we did exactly that We recently hosted a Manchester Digital member roundtable, bringing together employers to share how they are currently approaching AI use in technical interviews and, just as importantly, where they are still figuring things out The discussion brought together representatives from organisations including Arm, Auto Trader, Co-op, AJ Bell, the Information Commissioner’s Office, UK Biobank and others from across the Manchester Digital community

It is designed to create a high-trust environment for open, practical dialogue between those responsible for building, operating and regulating the infrastructure that underpins the UK’s digital economy. Attenda The forum is being convened by Manchester Digital , Cooperative Network Infrastructure , Greater Manchester Combined Authority and LINX , reflecting a shared commitment to strengthening the UK’s digital foundations and ensuring the benefits of growth are felt across the country The UK is entering a critical phase in the development of its digital infrastructure. Demand for compute capacity is accelerating rapidly as large language models and other data-intensive technologies become central to the digital economy At the same time, the country’s infrastructure remains heavily concentrated in London and the South East, where power capacity is increasingly constrained. Meanwhile, renewable energy generation in the North is often curtailed because it cannot be easily transported to where dema Northern cities, including Manchester, are home to major research institutions, established digital industries and fast-growing AI and innovation ecosystems. The strapline we’re carrying into MIPIM this week says it all: Manchester: Breaking Boundaries, Building Tomorrow Alongside this strapline we will be using a refreshed Invest Manchester identity across all our activity. We have made this change to reflect the city region’s ambitions and to bring together MIDAS and Marketing Manchester’s promotion activity. We want the world to be as excited Invest Manchester will be the single market facing identity for all our business investment work. Whether that is real estate, foreign direct investment, sector and place development, or international partnerships. Crucially, it will provide us with a collaborative platform, alon The investmanchester.co.uk website is live. We will work together with partners as we build content over the next six months, roll out our brand identity, and bring forward new campaigns We’re at a pivotal moment for the city region. Greater Manchester has built something remarkable. Average annual economic growth of 3.1% for the last ten years, over double the national average; 100,000 new homes delivered; an integrated public transport system; and population gr Over the last decade, we’ve attracted more foreign direct investment than any other UK regional city. candidates.evidence_text: The University of Salford, in collaboration with Innovate UK and Horizon EU V2X Cluster, brought together leading experts from across the UK and Europe to help shape the future of smart energy systems – at a time when the sector is moving from innovation to real-world delivery Hosted by Salford Business School, the UK-EU Innovation and Collaboration Symposium on Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) and Smart Charging welcomed around 100 participants from 70 organisations across 13 countries The event brought together voices from industry, government, research and community organisations to address one of the biggest challenges facing the energy transition: how to scale proven technologies into everyday use V2X allows electric vehicles to act as mobile energy assets – storing electricity and sharing it with homes, buildings and the wider grid This includes Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G), where vehicles can return electricity to the grid during peak demand, and smart charging, which optimises when vehicles charge to reduce costs and support renewable energy use Together, these technologies can help balance energy demand, lower costs and improve resilience. This is the first time the award has ever been presented to a student, after being opened t Gabriella (Gabby) Fee is studying on the University’s Nursing Associate Apprenticeship programme , and works for Alternative Futures Group, a health and social care charity providing specialist support for people with a learning disability or mental health need As an apprentice, Gabby divides her time between on-the-job training with her employer and learning at university. Her remarks set the tone for an event that asked us to consider Chaired by Dr Sally Penni MBE, Barrister, Founder of Women in the Law UK, the lecture was organised by Dr Ayesha Chowdhury. Ayesha’s expertise in counter-terrorism law aligned closely with the focus of this year’s lecture. Over the summer, Ayesha supervised students researching M In her lecture, ‘From Tragedy to Legislative Change: The Journey of Martyn’s Law’, Figen shared the story behind the campaign that led to the enactment of the Terrorism (Protection of Premises) Act 2025 The Act received Royal Assent in 2025 and introduces a statutory duty on those responsible for certain public premises and events to consider the risk of terrorist attacks and take proportionate steps to protect the public. candidates.evidence_text: Manchester Museum has officially opened its new exhibition, Human Natures , which examines our complex and often exploitative dynamic with the natural world, charting the various ways in which everyday choices around food, fashion and consumption have reshaped environments across Free to enter and running until Sun 1 Nov, the exhibition presents stories of overconsumption and environmental strain, confronting the consequences of ecological exploitation while spotlighting collective efforts to restore balance through more sustainable methods of living, wit The immersive experience also incorporates a striking installation from the Cottonopolis Collective, which explores the environmental legacy of cotton production. Textiles woven at Quarry Bank Mill and later buried have been transformed through exposure to soil, air and water, re Structured as a journey through four distinct settings, Human Natures guides visitors from a warehouse to a waste dump, chronicling the lifecycle of materials and the ever-shifting forms of our interactions with nature. Early sections explore domestication and agricultural change Then, the focus turns to clothing and the ethical implications of fashion through both historical and contemporary lenses. In this section, visitors can discover the remarkable story of Emily Williamson, whose campaign against the Victorian plumage trade began at her Fletcher Mos Elsewhere, the exhibition interrogates the realities of fast fashion and throw-away culture, particularly the imbalanced movement of textile waste across the planet. The work of Accra-based non-profit The Or Foundation highlights how discarded clothing from the Global North accum
candidates.draft_line: • В Manchester Museum открылась выставка Human Natures о связи человека с природой. Экспозиция охватывает темы потребления, моды и отходов, включая инсталляцию Cottonopolis Collective. Вход свободный, до 1 ноября.

candidates.evidence_text: Manchester Central Library , Manchester Until 20 December 2026 Additional information Always double check opening hours with the venue before making a special visit Manchester has always been a city with stories to tell. This guided walking tour will bring those stories to life. Led by a local expert, the experience is set to be a lively introduction to the people, places and ideas that have shaped one of the UK’s most influential cities The tour will begin at Manchester Central Library, that impossible to miss, grand circular landmark on St Peter’s Square. The neoclassical design has made it one of the city’s most-loved buildings, holding thousands of stories in its walls and on its shelves. The square itself ha From here, the tour takes you through the city’s heart, down the colourful streets of the Gay Village and the calming Sackville Gardens. It is here that you will see the monuments that honour Manchester’s role in the fight for equality and the city’s pioneering LGBTQ+ community a This guided tour is filled with monuments and tales from many moons ago. The Medieval Quarter is the perfect place to soak in all that history. It is here where you will see the oldest public library in the English-speaking world, Chetham’s Library . Housed in a beautiful sandsto During all this walking, your guide will point out local street art, hidden corners and stories you’d easily miss on your own. Tales of suffragettes, reformers, musicians and everyday Mancunians who’ve all left their mark on the city in their own way

The final afternoon of mjf 2026 takes over the Social at Aviva Studios – free to enter, no booking required – for a triple bill built around women and women-led songwriting, running alongside th Dilettante opens. The project of Leeds-based multi-instrumentalist Francesca Pidgeon – who moonlights in BC Camplight – makes the kind of art-pop that keeps finding new audiences without quite going mainstream, which is probably the point. Life of the Party , her first self-produ Bristol six-piece Caraway follow – jazz and neo-soul filtered through global folk, foley samples and double bass grooves, fronted by Polly Meyrick on vocals, saxophone and clarinet. Their reference points run from Bonobo to Ezra Collective, but their subject matter is more ground The afternoon’s centrepiece is Satnam Galsian, presenting the premiere of Love or Sacrifice – a new work co-commissioned by mjf with Oldham Coliseum Theatre and others. Galsian is a Leeds-based British Asian singer-songwriter with a background in North Indian classical music, and The Women in Jazz Photography exhibition – images of jazz captured by women – runs alongside all afternoon. Between the music and the walls, there’s plenty to explore. And the whole thing is free. For a festival that’s spent 10 days making a case for contemporary jazz’s range and

It’ This year’s headliners include China Moses, Andy Sheppard Trio, Yellowjackets, Toni Kofi and Denys Baptiste Quintet, Cassie Kinoshi’s seed., Bel Cobain, Lau Noah, NOUT, Cotonete and more. Across two stages, mjf will spotlight home-grown talent, artists developed through its own programmes and a wider mix of northern acts, with family-friendly activity From there, mjf radiates out across Manchester and beyond, taking in Matt & Phreds , Aviva Studios , RNCM , Stoller Hall , Low Four Studios , Flawd , Stage & Radio , The Carlton Club , Forsyth Music Shop, St Ann’s Church and Band on the Wall , where the festival rounds things off

' Demystifying AI and its Applications in Nuclear' will explore how AI is currently used and how it is planned to be used across bo The discussion will consider both the potential benefits and the limitations of AI, challenging perspectives on what it can and cannot deliver in a highly regulated and safety critical environment. Attention will be given to how AI can be implemented in a way that is ethical and There will be an talk followed by a Q&A, hosted by Dalton Champions Joshua Bettles and Ahmad Al-Fatly Dr Emily Collins - researcher in Computer Science looking at trustworthiness, responsibility, and accountability of AI agents with links to the nuclear industry and she is also part of CRADLE Daniel Braund - Senior AI Lead for Sellafield Ltd, working to apply AI and ML to improve safety, reduce cost and accelerate programmes across the nuclear facility lifecycle

It’s a treasure trove of one-of-a-kind handcrafted wonders, delicious food and unique finds, with something for everyone The Spinningfields Makers Market is dog-friendly , so bring your pup along to enjoy the fun. With doggy treats and pet-focused stalls, it’s the perfect day out for you and your four-legged companion LOCATION Spinningfields Square, Quay St, Manchester M3 3AP, UK Take a leisurely wander through the market, chat with the producers and don’t miss out on the chance to indulge in everything from artisanal cheeses to decadent chocolates, delightful tarts and an array of scrumptious street food options For an unforgettable day out, make sure you pop down and visit the Spinningfields Makers Market THE SPINNINGFIELDS MAKERS MARKET | Spinningfields ');mask-image:url('data:image/svg+xml;utf8, What is Pedddle? . Blog . Contact Us . Join Pedddle . The Market Success Guide . Login . Search . Search for: . Northern Quarter Makers Market. Second Sunday of every month Manchester, M4 5JD. Taking place on the second Sunday of every month in the heart of Manchester, the Northern Quarter Makers Market is a great market for an independent, edgy city vibe, with 95 stallholders showcasing their wares. . . . Details. Favourite Address Oak Street Manchester M4 5JD . Date Second Sunday of every month Time 11:00am - 5:00pm Next Markets 10th May 2026 , 14th Jun 2026 , 12th Jul 2026 , 9th Aug 2026 , 13th Sep 2026 , 11th Oct 2026 , 8th Nov 2026 , 13th Dec 2026 Organiser The Makers Market Social Website themakersmarket.co.uk/pages/northern-quarter-makers-market Accessibility Generally a flat market, wheelchair and pushchair accessible, but being in central Manchester please expect kerbs and the usual pedestrian/traffic. Parking Nearby city centre car parks available. We recommend arriving on public transport; the nearest tram stop is Shudehill (4 min walk), by Bus please use Shudehill Interchange (4 min walk), or if arriving by train, this market is just a 14 minute walk from Piccadilly (Cycle racks available). Facilities Wheelchair Accessible All Outdoor Seating Available Dog Friendly Free Entry Markets in Manchester , North West , Greater Manchester . The Northern Quarter Makers Market makes Manchester come alive with passionate creatives that are eager to share and inspire visitors with their award-winning food and drink, exhibiting awesome art, modern craft and vintage and live music. Northern Quarter Makers Market showcases around 95 stallholders, and continues to promise the creme de la creme when it comes to artisan traders in Manchester and the North West. A great place to visit if you want to shop independent in the city. They say ‘a weekend well spent brings a week of content’… so we’ll see you the in NQ!. Share this:. candidates.evidence_text: One of the most popular car boot sales in Greater Manchester returns in April Bowlee Car Boot Sale and Market – one of the most popular in Greater Manchester, attracting thousands of visitors every week, returns for the new season on Sunday, 5 April 2026 Organised by Rochdale Borough Council, the 2026 season at Bowlee Community Park in Middleton begins with an Easter bank holiday double on Sunday, 5 April 2026 and Monday, 6 April 2026 and then runs on Sundays and bank holiday Mondays right through until 11 October 2026 (excluding Sellers do not need to pre-book, and for the 8th year in a row prices have been frozen at £15 for cars, £20 for small vans/people carriers/4X4s and £25 for large vans and above. For buyers, there are parking spaces for more than 1,200 cars at £2.50 per vehicle Bowlee Car Boot Sale and Market is returning for its 16th year The event, now in its 16th year, takes a break on Sunday, 3 May 2026 to make way for Tour de Manc – the annual Greater Manchester charity bike ride that sets off from Bowlee Park with the choice of a 60-kilometre, 100-kilometre, 165-kilometre mile or 201-kilometre ride Up to 31 sales will take place this year and on 2 of the sales, parking for buyers will be reduced to £1. News and events . Your news . Bowlee is back - car boot sale and market returns for new season . . . Bowlee is back - car boot sale and market returns for new season . The new car boot season at Bowlee Community Park in Middleton begins on Sunday, 5 April 2026. Published: 3 March 2026 . . Bowlee Car Boot Sale and Market – one of the most popular in Greater Manchester, attracting thousands of visitors every week, returns for the new season on Sunday, 5 April 2026.. Organised by Rochdale Borough Council, the 2026 season at Bowlee Community Park in Middleton begins with an Easter bank holiday double on Sunday, 5 April 2026 and Monday, 6 April 2026 and then runs on Sundays and bank holiday Mondays right through until 11 October 2026 (excluding Sunday, 3 May 2026). . Sellers do not need to pre-book, and for the 8th year in a row prices have been frozen at £15 for cars, £20 for small vans/people carriers/4X4s and £25 for large vans and above. For buyers, there are parking spaces for more than 1,200 cars at £2.50 per vehicle.. Bowlee Car Boot Sale and Market is returning for its 16th year.. . ‘One of the busiest ever’. The event, now in its 16th year, takes a break on Sunday, 3 May 2026 to make way for Tour de Manc – the annual Greater Manchester charity bike ride that sets off from Bowlee Park with the choice of a 60-kilometre, 100-kilometre, 165-kilometre mile or 201-kilometre ride.. Up to 31 sales will take place this year and on 2 of the sales, parking for buyers will be reduced to £1. In response to customer feedback, if bad weather is forecast, a decision will be announced by 3pm the day before the sale, instead of the previous 6pm.. The event is one of the most popular in Greater Manchester, attracting thousands of visitors every week.. . Councillor Tricia Ayrton, cabinet member for climate change and environment, said:. “I am pleased this great event is returning to Bowlee Community Park for another year. We have an amazing following at Bowlee and last year was one of the busiest ever. Bargain hunters can dig out great deals and one-off items, and of course you can sell your own things too. People are a lot more conscious about recycling and re-selling now, so car boot sales are a great way of extending the life of items and reducing waste.”. View all the new dates and more details about the events . Bowlee Community Park is off Heywood Old Road (A6045), Middleton M24 4SB with easy access from the M60 (junction 19). Find Bowlee Community Park on Google Maps . Social media. Follow @BowleeCarBoot on Facebook . Search #BowleeCarBoot on Instagram . Related content. Councillor Tricia Ayrton . Whilst nearby Fallowfield and Withington cater to a grungier student crowd, Didsbury services a mixed bag of young professionals, families, retirees and, of course, their fashionable dogs- almost If you’re after cafes, Didsbury has them in droves. Our favourite is La Chouquette , a Parisian-style patisserie at the bottom of the highstreet. With a constantly rotating selection of desserts and pastries, at a reasonable-ish price, it’s easy to see why this place is so popula If you’re up for something a little more substantial, there are plenty of decent lunch options too. Nip up the road to Afti for their doorstop style focaccia sandwiches or stop in at A Front For Something , a delightfully whacky 80s themed cafe serving up familiar cafe fare at bu Looking for something with a little more whimsy? candidates.evidence_text: Congratulations to Khadija ‘Bunny’ Shaw, who has been voted the 2025/26 Etihad Player of the Season. The Jamaica international topped the five-player shortlist which also included Kerstin Casparij, Yui Hasegawa, Lauren Hemp and Vivianne Miedema It follows a fantastic campaign for Bunny who helped Andrée Jeglertz’s side win the Barclays Women’s Super League title and reach the Adobe Women’s FA Cup final, set to be played at the end of May Due to her phenomenal goalscoring exploits across the term, Shaw once again broke new ground in sky blue with her tally of 26 goals in 31 games etching her name in the history books By winning the 2026 WSL Golden Boot with her top-flight tally of 21 goals in 22 games, she won the prize for the third consecutive season - a league first – while she’s also the first player to reach 20 strikes in three different terms Of those 21 strikes, she bagged four in a 6-1 win over Aston Villa in December 2025 which saw her become the first women’s player to reach, and then surpass, 100 goals for City Already named FWA Football of the Year and also nominated for the WSL Player of the Season prize for 2025/26, the Jamaican surpassed 100 goal contributions in the competition with her two strikes in our 4-1 final day win over West Ham
